title: Confinement-controlled pattern selection in a finite population-imbalanced dipolar Bose-Einstein condensate
authors: Zhenhao Wang, Weijing Bao, Jia-Rui Luo, Gentaro Watanabe, Kui-Tian Xi
abstract: We study the ground-state density patterns of a population-imbalanced two-component dipolar Bose-Einstein condensate confined in a circular quasi-two-dimensional box. Using a mean-field model, we map out phase diagrams as functions of the axial confinement, interaction imbalance, and population ratio. The system supports a rich sequence of stationary morphologies, including a nearly uniform pancake state, pancake-droplet and ring-droplet coexistence states, droplet arrays, and concentric rings. These patterns show a close structural correspondence to microphase-separated morphologies in diblock-copolymer systems, with the population imbalance acting as an effective volume fraction that selects the pattern topology. Analysis of the density profiles and structure factors reveals that the modulated states possess an intrinsic nonzero characteristic wave vector, which remains essentially unchanged when the box size is varied. We also find that the characteristic pattern spacing scales linearly with the axial confinement length, indicating that the transverse thickness of the condensate controls the effective in-plane length scale. In a finite circular box, this smooth scaling is interrupted by discrete steps, reflecting geometric frustration and the integer locking of the number of rings or droplets. Our results show that box-trapped dipolar mixtures provide a controllable platform for studying finite-size pattern selection and nonlocal microphase formation in quantum fluids.
summary: This paper explores how axial confinement and population imbalance control the formation of complex density patterns in a two-component dipolar BEC. It demonstrates that these quantum patterns mimic microphase-separated structures in polymers and that their characteristic length scales are tunable via the confinement geometry.
main_problem: Investigating ground-state density pattern selection and morphological transitions in a population-imbalanced, two-component dipolar Bose-Einstein condensate confined in a circular quasi-2D box.
main_result: The study identifies a sequence of morphologies (pancake, droplet arrays, concentric rings) where the population ratio acts as an effective volume fraction, and discovers that the pattern spacing scales linearly with the axial confinement length.
method: A mean-field approach using coupled Gross-Pita_Pitaevskii equations (GPEs) minimized via the preconditioned conjugate-gradient method and evaluated using Fast Fourier Transforms.
model_or_system: A binary (two-component) dipolar Bose-Einstein condensate of 52Cr atoms in a quasi-2D circular box with tight axial harmonic confinement.
key_observables: 2D column density profiles, density contrast (C2), structure factors (radial and 2D Fourier power spectrum), and characteristic wave vectors.
important_parameters: Population ratio (N2/N), axial confinement strength (omega_z), interaction asymmetry (a22/a11), and box radius (R0).
main_assumptions: Zero temperature, mean-field approximation (neglecting Lee-Huang-Yang fluctuations), and rigid/hard-wall-like boundary conditions for the box potential.
figures_summary: Figures show real-space density patterns for different phases, density contrast (C2) as a function of population ratio and axial confinement, and scaling analysis of pattern spacing.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the system and its analogy to block copolymers, describes the mean-field model and numerical methods, presents phase diagrams and morphological transitions, analyzes scaling laws and geometric frustration, and concludes with implications for quantum fluid pattern selection.
why_interesting: It provides a controllable quantum fluid platform to study microphase separation and pattern formation, drawing a direct structural correspondence between quantum gases and soft-matter systems like diblock copolymers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Phase diagram in the (a22/a11, N2/N) plane for γ = 1, a11 = 100a0, a12 = a21 = 95a0, R0 = 20 µm, V0 = 100ℏω0, µ1 = 6µB, µ2 = −6µB, ωz = 5ω0, and total atom number N = 5 × 105. The color coding follows Fig. 1.

FIG. 1. (a) Phase diagram in the (ωz/ω0, N2/N) plane for γ = 1, a11 = 100a0, a22 = 90a0, a12 = a21 = 95a0, R0 = 20 µm, V0 = 100ℏω0, µ1 = 6µB, µ2 = −6µB, and total atom number N = 5 × 105. Colored regions indicate distinct density morphologies: pancake (P), pancake-droplet (PD), droplet (D), ring-droplet (RD), and concentric rings (CR). (b) Column-density profiles n2(ρ) at selected points in (a), labelled by markers. The field of view is 44.61 × 44.61 µm2. Density is plotted in units of N2ℓ−2.

FIG. 4. Robustness of the characteristic scale against system size. Comparison of real-space density, Fourier spectrum, and ra- dial structure factor for a larger box radius R0 = 40 µm. Parameters: ωz = 5ω0, N = 2 × 106, with N2/N = 0.32 (Droplets) and 0.5 (Rings). The selected k∗remains consistent with the R0 = 20 µm case in Fig. 3.

FIG. 3. (Color online) Characterization of microphase separa- tion. First column: Real-space density distribution. Second col- umn: Two-dimensional Fourier power spectrum F(kx, ky) in the range kx,y ∈[−2π/ℓ, 2π/ℓ]. The FFT satisfies Parseval’s theorem: R F(kρ)dkρ = R |δn2(ρ)|2dρ. Third column: One-dimensional radial structure factor S(kρ). The five rows correspond to the repre- sentative points marked in Fig. 1(a), with parameters (ωz/ω0, N2/N) = (5, 0.10), (5, 0.24), (5, 0.30), (5, 0.38), (5, 0.50) from top to bot- tom.

FIG. 5. Confinement-controlled scaling and geometric frustration. (a) Log-log plot of the mean inter-ring spacing ⟨d⟩(in units of ℓ) versus axial frequency ωz (in units of ω0). The solid line indicates the power-law fit ⟨d⟩∝ω−0.49 z . (b) Log-log plot of the droplet number Ndrop versus ωz. The step-like features indicate locking of the droplet number due to the finite box geometry.
